moves.text:
Hi frontend — backend here. I'll propose the whole v1 surface in one batch since the API is small: convention + 5 schemas (User, Message, MessageCreate, MessageList, Error) + 3 endpoints (POST /messages, GET /messages, GET /users/{id}).

Defaults I'm starting from (push back where it costs you something real):
- snake_case JSON keys (path of least resistance for our ORM; happy to flip to camelCase if rendering/codegen on your side benefits)
- Bearer JWT auth, inherited on all endpoints
- Short-polling for new messages via GET /messages?since=<cursor>; messages returned chronologically with next_cursor for the next poll. No SSE/WS in v1 unless you have a strong argument.
- Author is just an author_id ref; you fetch users via GET /users/{id} and cache. No embedded user objects on messages.
- Simple error envelope { error: { code, message } }
- No Idempotency-Key on POST /messages for v1 (real concern for double-tap; can add if you want)

Things I'd love your input on: cursor semantics for history vs. polling new (one endpoint with since/before, or split?), text length limit, whether you need batch user fetch.

display_name.description: Human-readable name shown in the UI.

id.description: Opaque message id; also usable as a cursor.

text.description: Message body. UTF-8, server-enforced max 4000 chars.

author_id.description: References User.id; the frontend caches users separately.

created_at.description: Server-assigned creation time, RFC 3339 / ISO 8601 UTC.

text.description: Message body. Author is derived from the bearer token.

next_cursor.description: Cursor to pass as `since` on the next poll. Null when there are no messages yet (cold start); otherwise always a string, even when no new messages arrived (use last seen).

message.description: Human-readable explanation; not stable, do not parse.

spec.description:
Returns messages in chronological order (oldest first).

- No `since`: returns the most recent `limit` messages (still ordered oldest-first within the page). Use this for an initial render.
- `since=<cursor>`: returns up to `limit` messages strictly newer than the cursor. Use this for polling.

`next_cursor` in the response is what you pass as `since` on the next call.

parameters.name: since
parameters.description: Cursor returned by a previous call's `next_cursor`. Omit for initial load.

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description: Max messages to return. Default 50, max 200.

200.description: A page of messages (possibly empty if polling and nothing new).

400.description: Invalid `since` cursor or `limit` out of range.

spec.description: Creates a message. The author is derived from the bearer token; do not send author_id in the body.

201.description: Created message; id, author_id, created_at are server-assigned.

400.description: Invalid body (e.g. empty text, text too long).

spec.description: Returns a single user. Intended to populate the frontend's user cache when an unknown author_id is seen.

moves.text:
Thanks — shape mostly works for us. One real ask: embed the author on Message. Our row renders need display_name+avatar_url inline; an N+1 user fetch per row (or even a batched lookup) is friction we'd rather not push into the client. I'm rejecting Message v1 and proposing v2 with author as an embedded User ref (keeping GET /users/{id} useful for profile views, just not on the render path).

Conceding the smaller stuff: snake_case is fine, JWT bearer is fine, short-poll with since-cursor is fine for now, 4000-char limit is fine, no batch user fetch needed if author embeds. One small follow-up worth considering on POST /messages: an optional Idempotency-Key header would let us safely retry on flaky network without dup messages — happy to defer if you'd rather not, since dedup can also live on the client.

moves.name: Message
moves.reason: embed author (display_name+avatar_url) on the message so a chat row renders without an extra fetch; counter in v2

id.description: Opaque message id; also usable as a cursor.

text.description: Message body. UTF-8, server-enforced max 4000 chars.

author.description: Embedded author so a chat row renders without an extra fetch.

created_at.description: Server-assigned creation time, RFC 3339 / ISO 8601 UTC.

moves.reason: snake_case + JWT bearer fine; ORM ergonomics on your side beat our codegen ergonomics on ours
still useful for profile views even with author embedded on Message

moves.text: Left MessageList / GET /messages / POST /messages in proposed (your originals) deliberately — they ref Message and the doc validator wouldn't let me accept until Message resolves. Once you take Message v2 (or counter), I'll accept those three immediately; no other changes needed on them as far as I can see.

moves.name: Message
moves.reason: Fair trade — single JOIN against the users table on our side beats N+1 (or batched fetch) on the render path. Users table is small reference data so the extra bytes per message are fine. Note for both of us: embedded author is a point-in-time snapshot at fetch; if a user later edits display_name/avatar, previously-rendered messages keep the old values until refetched (same as client-side caching, just no inconsistency window between message and user).

moves.text:
Took Message v2 — embedding author is fine, single JOIN on our side beats N+1 on yours. As you noted, MessageList / GET /messages / POST /messages should validate now against the originals; nothing else to change there from me.

On Idempotency-Key: deferring for v1 as you offered. Keeps the contract tight and client-side dedup covers the flaky-network case. If we see real duplicate-message reports in production we'll add it post-v1 — easy additive change (optional header, no breaking impact).

I think we're effectively done once you accept the remaining three. Handing back to you.

moves.name: MessageList
moves.reason: LGTM; chronological pagination + next_cursor semantics are clear, nullable cold-start case is well-documented.
Polling shape works for v1; cursor semantics (omit for initial, since= for poll) are clean.

Send shape works. Tiny nit (not blocking): description says 'id, author_id, created_at are server-assigned' but Message v2 has 'author' (embedded) not author_id — feel free to tidy in a v2 of this op or leave it, the schema ref is unambiguous.

spec.description: A single chat message, with the author embedded so a client can render a chat row from one GET /messages response (no separate users lookup). The embedded `author` reflects the user's *current* profile as of the GET — the server resolves it via a join on users.id at fetch time, it is not a stored snapshot. Practical consequence: if a user changes their display_name or avatar_url, the next poll will return the new values on their historical messages as well.

id.description: Stable opaque message id. Lexicographically orderable (ULID-style) so the client can use it directly as the `since` cursor on GET /messages.

text.description: Message body. UTF-8, no server-side formatting. When `deleted_at` is non-null the server may clear or substitute this field; clients should render a tombstone based on `deleted_at` rather than displaying `text` for deleted messages.

author.description: The author User as of this GET. Server-resolved via join on users.id = messages.author_id; no denormalized snapshot is stored.

created_at.description: ISO 8601 UTC timestamp of when the server accepted the message.

edited_at.description: ISO 8601 UTC timestamp of the most recent edit, or null if the message has never been edited. Clients may show an "(edited)" affordance when this is non-null.

deleted_at.description: ISO 8601 UTC soft-delete timestamp. Null for live messages. When set, the message is a tombstone — clients should render "[deleted]" or similar in place of `text`.

spec.description: Body for PATCH /messages/{id}. Replaces the message's text. Author identity comes from the bearer token; no fields beyond `text` are settable from the client.

text.description: Replacement message body. UTF-8, server-enforced max 4000 chars, non-empty.

spec.description: Snapshot of who is currently typing in the chat, as of the time of the GET /typing call. "Currently" is server-defined — typically users whose last heartbeat (POST /typing) arrived within an idle window of a few seconds. Embeds full User objects (mirroring the Message.author embedding pattern) so a client can render "Alice and Bob are typing…" without a follow-up users lookup.

typing_users.description: Users currently typing. Excludes the requesting user (a client never needs to render its own typing indicator back to itself). Order is not significant.

spec.description: Replace the text of a message you authored. Allowed only within a server-defined edit window (suggest 5 minutes) from `created_at`; outside the window the server returns 403. On success the server sets `edited_at` to now and returns the updated Message. The edit also needs to propagate to other clients viewing the thread — see the rationale message on this proposal for the open cursor-semantics question on GET /messages.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: Message id (as returned by POST /messages or GET /messages).

400.description: Invalid body (empty text, text too long).

403.description: Not the message author, or edit window has expired.

spec.description: Soft-delete a message you authored. Allowed only within a server-defined deletion window (suggest 5 minutes) from `created_at`; outside the window the server returns 403. On success the server sets `deleted_at` to now (and may clear `text`) and returns the tombstone Message. Returning the tombstone body (vs. 204) lets the client patch its in-memory thread immediately without a refetch.

403.description: Not the message author, or deletion window has expired.

sideEffects: updates messages row (deleted_at, possibly clears text)

spec.description: Tell the server the authenticated user is currently typing. Clients should debounce this — sending one heartbeat every 2-3 seconds while the input field is non-empty and being modified is plenty. The server records a presence timestamp keyed by user id; GET /typing surfaces users whose most-recent heartbeat is within the idle window. No body; identity comes from the bearer token. Fire-and-forget — the client does not need the response value for anything.

sideEffects: updates in-memory presence map (last_typed_at by user_id)

spec.description: Returns the set of users whose most-recent typing heartbeat (POST /typing) is within the server-defined idle window. Polled separately from GET /messages so the client can use a tighter cadence (typically every ~2 seconds) without coupling it to the message poll interval. Excludes the requesting user.

moves.text: Core v1 fully accepted — thanks. Loading in the two product extras you hadn't seen yet (design signed off on both): typing indicator (POST/GET /typing) and 5-minute edit/delete (PATCH/DELETE /messages/{id} + Message v3 adds nullable edited_at/deleted_at). Open question: propagation of edits/deletes through GET /messages. Current poll semantics ('strictly newer than cursor') won't surface modified messages, so other clients won't see edits/deletes until a hard refresh. Two paths I see: (a) extend the cursor to mean 'messages whose id > since OR whose edited_at/deleted_at > cursor_time' — single endpoint, slightly more complex query; (b) separate change-event endpoint. Your call — you've got the DB-side context. Happy to take a Message v4 / GET /messages v2 counter-proposal if you want to tighten the semantics. Handing back.

moves.name: Message
moves.reason: Edit/delete is a reasonable v1 addition; tombstone-on-success (vs 204) avoids a refetch, soft-delete columns are normal DB work, and the embedded-author clarification (server-resolved join, not snapshot) matches what we'd do anyway.

moves.name: MessageEdit
moves.reason: Edit/delete is a reasonable v1 addition; tombstone-on-success (vs 204) avoids a refetch, soft-delete columns are normal DB work, and the embedded-author clarification (server-resolved join, not snapshot) matches what we'd do anyway.
5-minute window is cheap to enforce (created_at + interval check on the row); 403 vs 404 split is correct; returning the updated/tombstone Message keeps client state coherent without a refetch.
5-minute window is cheap to enforce (created_at + interval check on the row); 403 vs 404 split is correct; returning the updated/tombstone Message keeps client state coherent without a refetch.

moves.name: TypingStatus
moves.reason: Typing is a push-shaped signal getting forced into poll — and we agreed to no push channel in v1. Two real costs on our side: (1) sustained background load from every connected client GET /typing'ing every ~2s plus heartbeat POSTs (~constant traffic even in an idle room), and (2) the UX is fundamentally degraded — 2s poll = 2s lag on an indicator that wants to feel instant, and tighter cadence makes the load problem worse. The right home for typing is when we add SSE/WS (v1.x), at which point it becomes a first-class push event and the operational cost is right-sized. Deferring.

Deferring with TypingStatus v1 — see that rejection for the full rationale. Tl;dr: typing wants a push channel, we agreed no push in v1, and poll-based typing is bad UX (lag) and bad cost (sustained traffic in idle rooms). Right home for this is the SSE/WS round.

Deferring with TypingStatus v1 — see that rejection for the full rationale. Tl;dr: typing wants a push channel, we agreed no push in v1, and poll-based typing is bad UX (lag) and bad cost (sustained traffic in idle rooms). Right home for this is the SSE/WS round.

spec.summary: List messages / poll for new, edited, and deleted
spec.description:
Returns messages in change-time order. Powers both initial render and polling,
including propagation of edits and deletes from PATCH/DELETE /messages/{id}.

- **No `since`** (initial render): returns the most recent `limit` messages,
  chronologically (oldest first within the page). Tombstones (`deleted_at`
  set) are included so the client renders the same view as anyone else
  looking at the thread.

- **`since=<cursor>`** (polling): returns up to `limit` messages whose state
  has changed since the cursor — newly created, edited, or soft-deleted.
  Ordered ascending by *change-time* (server-side `max(created_at,
  edited_at, deleted_at)` per row), so `next_cursor` advances monotonically
  and a single page never straddles two state changes of the same row.

**Dedupe by `id`.** A previously-seen message can reappear when it is edited
or deleted; clients should treat the response as an upsert stream keyed by
`id`, replacing any in-memory copy with the row returned.

**Cursor is opaque.** It encodes a server-side change-time, not an id. Always
pass back the `next_cursor` from your previous response; do not synthesize a
cursor from a message id.

parameters.name: since
parameters.description: Opaque cursor from a previous call's `next_cursor`. Omit for initial load.

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description: Max messages to return. Default 50, max 200.

200.description: A page of messages (possibly empty if polling and nothing has changed). May include tombstones (`deleted_at` set) and edited rows (`edited_at` set).

400.description: Invalid `since` cursor or `limit` out of range.

moves.text:
Round summary:

Took the edit/delete stack — Message v3, MessageEdit v1, PATCH /messages/{id}, DELETE /messages/{id}. 5-min window is cheap server-side, soft-delete columns are bog standard, and returning the tombstone/edited row from the mutations keeps your in-memory thread coherent.

Rejected the typing trio (TypingStatus, POST /typing, GET /typing). Not the feature — the transport. Typing-over-poll is a sustained-cost / high-latency combo that's bad on both ends, and we explicitly didn't sign up for a push channel in v1. Right home for typing is the SSE/WS round (call it v1.x). When we add that, typing falls out for free as a push event with the cost shaped correctly.

For propagation of edits/deletes through GET /messages: proposed v2 going with your option (a) — single endpoint, extended semantics. Key changes:
- 'since' now means 'changed since', not 'newer than': returns rows where max(created_at, edited_at, deleted_at) > cursor-time.
- Ordered ascending by change-time so next_cursor advances monotonically.
- Cursor is opaque and encodes a server-side change-time, not a message id — clients must use next_cursor (id-as-cursor only ever surfaced new rows, won't see edits).
- Clients dedupe by id since a previously-seen message can reappear in modified form.
- Initial render (no since) unchanged in shape — most recent limit, oldest first within the page, includes tombstones so everyone sees the same thread state.

DB-side: index on greatest(created_at, edited_at, deleted_at) covers it. The query gets one extra OR clause; not a meaningful complexity bump.

If v2 looks right, accept and we're done with v1. If you want a separate change-feed endpoint instead I'll take a counter, but I think one endpoint with upsert-stream semantics is cleaner than two polls running side by side.

moves.reason: v2 matches option (a) exactly — change-time semantics, opaque cursor, upsert-stream dedupe by id, tombstones in initial render. The doc copy is unambiguous about cursor opacity and replace-on-id, which is what we need for the in-memory thread to stay coherent. p50 25ms / p99 180ms is a fine bump for the extra clause. v1 closed on our side.

moves.text:
Round close from our side: accepted GET /messages v2 — change-time cursor + upsert-stream-keyed-by-id is exactly what edit/delete propagation needed; doc copy is clear enough that I don't think we'll trip over the cursor-opacity rule in client code.

On the typing trio: taking the deferral. You're right that typing-over-poll is shape-wrong — sustained traffic for an almost-always-empty signal, and worst-case staleness pinned to the poll interval. Punting to the v1.x SSE/WS round is the better home. Logging here so it doesn't get lost: when push lands, we want a typing event ({ thread_id, user_id, started_at } or similar — shape TBD then) emitted while a user is actively typing, debounced server-side, no separate read endpoint. I'll re-propose against the push surface in that round, not against v1.

v1 looks done on our side. Thanks — clean negotiation.
